* Integrating the Google Maps Platform Routes API for route generation and traffic-aware navigation.  
* Integrating the Google Maps Platform Places API for location-based services and charging station retrieval.  
* Collecting route distance, travel time, and traffic condition data from external APIs.  
* Processing route elevation information and geographic data.  
* Developing the localized EV range prediction model.  
* Implementing the weighted energy consumption formula:  
  ***E \= Ebase x Wtraffic x Welevation x Wtemperature***  
  Where:  
* E \= Estimated energy consumption  
* Ebase \= Baseline energy consumption derived from manufacturer specifications (Wh/km)  
* Wtraffic \= Traffic congestion adjustment weight  
* Welevation \= Road elevation adjustment weight  
* Wtemperature \= Ambient temperature adjustment weight

The model estimates battery energy consumption using weighted adjustment factors that reflect real-world Philippine driving conditions. Traffic congestion, terrain elevation, and ambient temperature are incorporated to provide a more realistic estimate of EV energy usage compared to traditional distance-based calculations.  
To improve prediction accuracy, the coefficient weights will be calibrated using collected route and driving data. The system will also calculate the estimated remaining battery State-of-Charge (SoC) and projected driving range throughout the route.

Deliverables:

* Google Maps API integration  
* EV range prediction engine  
* Energy consumption calculation module  
* State-of-Charge (SoC) estimation module  
* Traffic, elevation, and temperature data integration

**Phase 3: Core Algorithm and API Integration (Sprints 5–6)**  
This sprint focuses on geographic information and charging station features.  
Activities include:

* Integration of Google Maps Platform APIs.  
* Display of interactive maps.  
* Charging station retrieval and visualization.  
* Geographic filtering using PostGIS.  
* Radius-based charging station searches.

Deliverables:

* Interactive map interface  
* Charging station locator module  
* Geospatial query services

**Phase 4: Intelligent Routing and Charging Integration (Sprints 7–8)**  
This phase focuses on route optimization and charging station accessibility.  
Activities include:

* Developing energy-efficient route recommendations.  
* Implementing traffic-aware routing.  
* Integrating charging station data into the navigation system.  
* Creating charging station search and filtering functions.  
* Implementing radius-based charging station lookups using PostGIS.  
* Developing charging-aware route suggestions.  
* Displaying reachable travel zones and route alternatives.

Deliverables:

* Route optimization module  
* Charging station locator  
* Intelligent navigation system

**Phase 5: System Refinement and Testing (Sprints 9–10)**  
The final phase focuses on testing, optimization, and system preparation.  
Activities include:

* Conducting unit testing.  
* Conducting integration testing.  
* Conducting system testing.  
* Performing user acceptance testing.  
* Fixing identified bugs and issues.  
* Optimizing application performance.  
* Preparing the application for deployment.  
* Conducting controlled route validation tests.

The accuracy of the EV range prediction model will be evaluated using:

* Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE)  
* Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)

Deliverables:

* Test reports  
* Bug resolution documentation  
* Final application release

**Sprint Timeline**

| Sprint | Major Activities |
| :---- | :---- |
| Sprint 1-2 | Project Setup, UI/UX Development |
| Sprint 3-4 | Database Design, Backend Development, and Core System Architecture setup |
| Sprint 5-6 | Google Maps API Integration, EV Range Prediction Model Development, and SoC Estimation Module |
| Sprint 7-8 | Intelligent Routing, Charging Station Integration, and Geospatial Processing using PostGIS |
| Sprint 9-10 | System Testing, Model Validation (MAPE & RMSE), Optimization, Bug Fixing, and Deployment Preparation |

**Sprint Deliverables**

| Sprint | Major Activities |
| :---- | :---- |
| Sprint 1-2 | UI Prototype, Navigation Screens |
| Sprint 3-4 | Database Schema, Backend APIs |
| Sprint 5-6 | EV Prediction Engine, SoC Module |
| Sprint 7-8 | Route Optimization Module, Charging Station Locator |
| Sprint 9-10 | Test Reports, Validation Results, Final Release |

**Technologies and Development Tools**  
The project will utilize the following technologies:

| Component | Technology |
| :---- | :---- |
| Mobile Application | React Native with Expo (TypeScript) |
| Web/Admin Frontend | React \+ TypeScript \+ Vite |
| Backend Application Layer | [Node.js](http://Node.js) \+ Express |
| Programming Language | TypeScript |
| Database Management | PostgreSQL |
| Cloud Database Hosting | Supabase |
| Geospatial processing | PostGIS |
| Mapping Services | Google Maps Platform (Routes API and Places API) |
| Version Control  | Git and Github |
| API Testing | Postman |
| Project Management | Github Projects |

**Version Control and Collaboration**  
Git will serve as the primary version control system, while GitHub will be used as the centralized repository for project collaboration and source code management.  
The development team will follow a feature-branch workflow where individual features, bug fixes, and integrations are developed in isolated branches before being reviewed and merged into the main branch. Pull requests will be utilized to conduct code reviews and maintain software quality.  
Project progress will be monitored using GitHub Projects, where sprint backlogs, assigned tasks, and development milestones will be tracked throughout the project lifecycle. Daily standup meetings, sprint planning sessions, sprint reviews, and sprint retrospectives will be conducted regularly to ensure alignment with project objectives and continuous improvement of development processes.  
Through Agile Scrum, structured sprint planning, collaborative development practices, and modern cloud-based technologies, VoltPath PH can be developed systematically while ensuring flexibility, scalability, and continuous quality improvement throughout the project lifecycle.

		

4. # **Testing Plan** {#testing-plan}

	The testing strategy for VoltPath PH is designed to validate system correctness, prediction accuracy, and usability across all layers of the application. Five testing types will be conducted across Sprints 9–10 and during the controlled road-test phase. Table 7 summarizes each testing type alongside its scope and success criteria.

*Table 7\. Testing Plan Summary*

| Testing Type | Scope | Success Criteria |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Unit Testing | Individual functions within the energy prediction engine, SoC calculator, route weight scoring, and PostGIS query helpers. | All unit tests pass with ≥ 90% code coverage on core calculation modules. |
| Integration Testing | End-to-end API flows: trip optimization endpoint, charging station retrieval, Google Maps API data pipeline, and database read/write operations. | All API endpoints return correct HTTP status codes and response payloads for both valid and invalid inputs. |
| System Testing | Full application stack deployed to Railway staging environment: mobile-to-API-to-database round trips, map rendering, and route display. | All functional requirements (FR-01 through FR-10) verified across both Android and iOS builds. |
| Model Validation (Route Tests) | Controlled real-world test drives on four predefined route types in Metro Manila and Cavite using the Geely EX5 Em-i Max. | Prediction model achieves MAPE ≤ 15% and RMSE ≤ 80 Wh/km against recorded SoC values. |
| User Acceptance Testing (UAT) | Internal evaluation conducted by the three researchers, each independently completing predefined task scenarios (trip planning, charging station lookup, SoC interpretation). | Average System Usability Scale (SUS) score ≥ 70 across all three researchers; unanimous rating of interface as at least easy to use. |

**Unit Testing**

Unit tests will be written using Jest for the backend and React Testing Library for frontend components. The energy prediction engine functions, including the multiplicative weighting formula, SoC depletion calculator, and route segment energy aggregator, will be tested with known input-output pairs derived from the test-drive dataset. A minimum of 90% code coverage is required for core calculation modules before advancing to integration testing.

**Integration Testing**

Integration tests will validate the full data pipeline from API endpoint to external service to database and back. Key integration scenarios include: (1) the trip optimization endpoint correctly calling the Google Maps Routes and Elevation APIs and applying energy weighting factors; (2) the charging station locator correctly executing PostGIS radius queries and returning filtered results by plug type; and (3) user authentication and JWT token validation across protected endpoints. Postman collections will be used to automate these endpoint-level tests during each sprint review.

**System Testing**

System tests will execute the full application stack on the Railway staging environment, verifying that mobile-to-API-to-database round trips succeed for all ten functional requirements defined in Table 3\. System tests will be run on both Android and iOS builds to confirm cross-platform consistency. Any failures identified during system testing will be logged in GitHub Issues, prioritized in the sprint backlog, and resolved before the UAT phase.

**Model Validation — Route Test Drives**

The accuracy of the EV range prediction model will be evaluated through controlled road tests on the Geely EX5 Em-i Max across the four predefined route types (Table 1). For each route segment, the predicted energy consumption generated by VoltPath PH will be compared against the actual energy consumption computed from recorded SoC readings. Model accuracy will be measured using:

* Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE): target ≤ 15%.  
* Root Mean Square Error (RMSE): target ≤ 80 Wh/km.

If MAPE or RMSE targets are not met after initial calibration, the regression analysis will be re-run with an expanded dataset and the weighting factors will be recalibrated. Route tests will be conducted during both peak and off-peak periods to ensure the model generalizes across traffic conditions.

**User Acceptance Testing**

UAT will be conducted internally by the three researchers who developed the system. Each researcher will assume the role of an end user and independently complete a set of predefined task scenarios using the VoltPath PH mobile application, including planning a trip with a low starting SoC, identifying charging stations along a route, and interpreting the SoC display at each waypoint. After completing the tasks, each researcher will accomplish the System Usability Scale (SUS) questionnaire as an evaluator. A minimum average SUS score of 70 across the three researchers and a unanimous rating of the interface as at least easy to use are required for the system to be considered acceptable for deployment. Findings from this internal UAT will be used to identify usability issues for resolution prior to final submission

**Bug Tracking and Resolution**

All defects identified during any testing phase will be logged in GitHub Issues with a severity classification of Critical, High, Medium, or Low. Critical and High severity bugs block the next sprint review and must be resolved before proceeding. Medium and Low severity issues are added to the sprint backlog and addressed during the Sprint 9–10 refinement cycle. Resolved bugs will be verified through regression tests before their GitHub Issues are closed.

5. # **Implementation Plan** {#implementation-plan}

**Deployment Architecture**  
VoltPath PH will be deployed to Railway, a cloud platform that natively supports Turborepo monorepo structures. The railway.json file at the repository root defines three Railway services: api (the Node.js Express backend), web (the React web application), and a managed PostgreSQL service with the PostGIS extension enabled. The mobile application will be distributed via Expo Application Services (EAS) for internal testing builds during the evaluation period, with final builds submitted to the Google Play Store and Apple App Store upon project completion.

**Environment Configuration**  
Each deployment environment , development, staging, and production, will maintain separate environment variable sets. Required variables include the Google Maps Platform API Key (scoped to Routes, Places, and Elevation APIs), the Supabase database connection string, the JWT secret, and the Node environment flag. Sensitive credentials will never be committed to the repository; they will be injected via Railway's environment variable management panel and referenced in the application through a typed configuration module within packages/shared.

**Hardware and Software Requirements**  
Development team members will require machines capable of running Node.js 18+, the Android Emulator or Xcode iOS Simulator, and the Expo Go application on a physical test device. The Geely EX5 Em-i Max test vehicle will be used for the real-world route validation test drives. A mobile device with GPS capability will be used to log route coordinates and timestamps during test drives. The production infrastructure runs entirely on cloud services: Railway for compute and Supabase for the database — requiring no dedicated physical servers.

**User Training and Documentation**  
In-app onboarding will guide new users through selecting their EV model, entering a trip, and interpreting the range and SoC displays during their first session. A help screen accessible from the main navigation will provide brief explanations of each key feature. Technical documentation covering the API, database schema, and deployment procedures will be maintained in the /docs directory of the GitHub repository, including BACKEND.md, FRONTEND\_MOBILE.md, FRONTEND\_WEB.md, DEVOPS.md, ARCHITECTURE.md, and ONBOARDING.md files already scaffolded in the repository.

**Post-Deployment Maintenance**  
Following the initial deployment, the team will monitor application performance and error rates via Railway's built-in logging and metrics. Google Maps Platform API usage will be tracked against quota limits through the Google Cloud Console. Charging station data currency will be maintained through the crowdsourced user reporting feature (FR-10) and periodic manual verification against the EVRO Philippines platform and DOE-published station lists. The EV model database will be expanded to include additional Philippine-market EV models as the sector grows under EVIDA. User feedback collected through the in-app report mechanism and UAT sessions will be reviewed at the end of each sprint to identify improvement priorities for the next development cycle.

**Conclusion**  
The methodology presented in this chapter provides a structured, evidence-based approach to the design, development, and evaluation of VoltPath PH. By grounding the energy prediction model in controlled real-world driving data collected on Philippine roads, adopting an Agile Scrum workflow that enables continuous validation across ten development sprints, and defining clear quantitative success criteria for both system accuracy (MAPE ≤ 15%, RMSE ≤ 80 Wh/km) and usability (SUS ≥ 70), this methodology directly addresses the identified gap in localized EV navigation support within the Philippines. Together, the requirement analysis, system design, development plan, testing plan, and implementation plan establish the technical and procedural foundation necessary for VoltPath PH to deliver reliable, locally-calibrated EV range prediction and intelligent route optimization to Filipino EV drivers.

**Appendix B: Disclosure of Artificial Intelligence Tools Used in Research**

 **Disclosure of Artificial Intelligence Tools Used in Research**

The authors of this paper, *\<First Name, MI, Last Name\>*, from section \<*Insert section*\>, enrolled in *\<MO-IT200D1 \- Capstone 1\>* for Term *\<1, 2, or 3\>*, under the supervision of their adviser, *\<Mentor/Adviser’s First Name, MI, Last Name\>*, disclose the use of the following Artificial Intelligence (AI) Research tools in this Capstone Paper, titled *\<Insert title\>*, that is supportive and ethical, consistent to the [guidelines](https://support.mmdc.mcl.edu.ph/hc/en-us/articles/37180248710541-MMDC-s-Position-on-the-Use-of-AI-Tools) issued by the school:

| AI Tool  | Specific Use/Purpose   | AI-aided Sections/Chapters |
| ----- | ----- | ----- |
| *ChatGPT* | *To improve sentence construction*  | *Chapter 1 \- Significance of the Study* |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |
|  |  |  |

Further, this is to certify the following:

1. No AI tools were used to generate original arguments, research gap analysis, hypothesis, data analysis, conclusions, or recommendations. The authors understand that thesis/feasibility and capstone projects showcase original content.   
2. No confidential and/or privileged information was uploaded for AI use or training, including data collected, interview clips, or any other similar items.   
3. No AI-generated content was fully lifted and without verification. The authors recognize the supportive nature of AI Research tools and certify their ethical use.   
4. AI Research tools used are limited and supportive, e.g., enhancement of sentence structure, improving phrasing, etc.    
5. If requested by the faculty adviser, attachments of the full prompts and responses of the AI tool shall be included in the appendix. 

The authors assume full responsibility and accountability for the accuracy, originality, and quality whether for course requirements of this course, publication, or any other academic use bearing the name of both the authors and the institution. 

Signed:

\<Insert authors’ names and signatures\>

Conforme:  
\<Insert mentor’s name and signature\>

*\[Delete the texts in red before submission\]*

*Note to the authors:*  
Include this declaration to the Appendix section. Include this step as part of the ethical considerations, methodology, and other chapters wherever necessary. 

**Appendix C: Interview Questions**

1. Note that each appendix must be on a separate page.   
2. Make sure to label them properly as seen above.   
3. Appendices are the supplementary materials that support the capstone project but are not included in the main body of the document. These include raw data, charts, survey questionnaires, interview transcripts, financial tables, and any additional documents referenced in the study. Ensure that there is no disclosure of personal information in your raw data (e.g., participant’s name, company, etc.) by assigning labels or using pseudonyms.  
4. Make sure that one of your appendices is the **Certificate of Proofreading** when you’ve been required by your panelists for proofreading by a qualified proofreader.
